sections.heading: Data Classes
sections.content: A data class is a class whose primary purpose is to hold data. When you mark a class with the data keyword, the compiler automatically generates several useful functions.
sections.code: data class User(val id: Int, val name: String, val email: String)

sections.heading: Requirements for Data Classes
items: The primary constructor must have at least one parameter.
All primary constructor parameters must be marked as val or var.
Data classes cannot be abstract, open, sealed, or inner.

sections.heading: Automatically Generated Functions
sections.content: When using a data class, Kotlin automatically generates useful methods for you.

sections.heading: What You Get Automatically
items: toString(): Returns a readable representation of the object.
equals() and hashCode(): Compare objects based on their data.
copy(): Creates a new object with modified properties.

sections.heading: copy() Example
sections.content: The copy() function allows you to create a modified version of an existing object while keeping immutability.
sections.code:
val user1 = User(1, "Samy", "samy@mail.com")

val user2 = user1.copy(email = "new_samy@mail.com")

sections.heading: Destructuring Declarations
sections.content: Data classes allow you to unpack an object into multiple variables. This is commonly used when handling state or results.

sections.heading: Enum Classes
sections.content: Enum classes represent a fixed set of constants, such as directions, states, or difficulty levels.

sections.heading: Enums with Properties
sections.content: Enums in Kotlin can define properties and functions, making them more powerful than simple constants.

sections.heading: Using Enums with when
sections.content: The when expression works very well with enums. If all enum cases are handled, no else branch is required.

sections.heading: Summary
items: data class removes boilerplate for data-holding classes.
copy() allows safe updates of immutable objects.
enum class represents a fixed set of constants.
Enums can contain properties and functions.
